We need to produce JSON with files array. The stage is lib-001-core. The expected artifacts: library-chain/core (project). The validator: core-builds: failure_class=build, core-sample-runs: failure_class=runtime_behavior. So we need to create a library project and a sample caller that runs. The sample caller must run and produce expected output. The expected output likely "Hello from Lib". So we need to ensure that the sample prints that. The library must have a public function Greet returning that string. The sample must reference the library. So we need to create two projects: core library and core-sample. The library project path: library-chain/core/CoreLibrary.csproj. The library code file: library-chain/core/Lib.cs. The sample project: library-chain/core-sample/CoreSample.csproj. The sample code: library-chain/core-sample/Program.cs.

We need to produce JSON with these files. The content must be correct. The content strings must be properly escaped. The JSON content strings can contain newlines as 
. The double quotes inside content must be escaped as ". The backslashes inside path must be escaped as \. For example, in Program.cs, we have no double quotes. In Lib.cs, we have "Hello from Lib" which must be escaped. In CoreSample.csproj, we have Include="..\core\CoreLibrary.csproj" which must be escaped as Include=\"..\\core\\CoreLibrary.csproj\". So the content string for CoreSample.csproj must be:

"<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net6.0</TargetFramework>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\\core\\CoreLibrary.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>"

But we need to escape backslashes inside JSON string: each backslash must be doubled. So the string inside JSON will have ..\\core\\CoreLibrary.csproj. But we need to escape the backslashes in the JSON string. So the content string will be:

</Project>"
